text: The lung has been flipped posteriorly and suspended with the rampley's forceps.
This allowed clear visualisation of the right lower lobe inferior pulmonary vein.
The lateral basal and posterior basal lower lobe segmental veins
which can be seen branching off the right inferior lobe vein, were then gently isolated with a blunt dissection using a combination of the lymph node grasper and metallic suction.
The first vein was stapled with a tri-stapler.
Note the use of a curved tip stapler to allow gentle passage of the stapling device posterior to the segmental vein.
and due to the smaller size, the second vein is shown being carefully cauterized with a Covidian ligature.
Here we can see the anterior basal vein remaining intact following division of the branches draining segments 9 and segments 10.
The lung was then allowed to drop down anteriorly, commencing the second part of this procedure, dissection of the fissure and exposure of the pulmonary artery.

Please note the staple line on the right-hand side of the screen demonstrating a retrospection of the right middle lobe that has already been performed within this procedure, done to remove the previously mentioned nodule on the CT scan.
On careful inspection, the pulmonary artery can be seen here within the fissure.
The overlying pulmonary visceral pleura was then opened using ligature electrocautery.
Careful blunt dissection was utilised in this area due to the close proximity of the pulmonary artery.
The incision was then extended using a tri-stapler to further expose the pulmonary artery.
Further blunt dissection was performed to enable visualization and distinguishing of the different branches.
In this view, the posterior basal and the lateral basal right pulmonary arteries can be distinguished from the others due to the branching pattern.
Roberts was then used to allow passage of a sloop around the segment 9 and segment 10 pulmonary artery.
This allows gentle manipulation to enable passage of a curved stapler.
The lateral basal and posterior basal artery common trunk was then stapled off and inspected for any residual bleeding.
The third part of the procedure involved isolation and division of the lateral basal and posterior basal segmental bronchi.
To enable access, the lung was again suspended posteriorly.
The bronchus can be seen here on the right of the inferior lobe pulmonary vein.
The lateral basal and posterior basal segmental bronchus was then isolated before it divided into the separate branches going to each segment.
Careful blunt dissection was required here due to the close proximity of the inferior lobe pulmonary vein and utilised a combination of the lymph node grasper and metallic suction.
Another curve tip stapler was used here to allow passage of the tristapler behind the segment 9 and segment 10 segmental bronchi.
Prior to stapling, an inflation test was performed to demonstrate inflation of the non-isolated lung segments.
The stapling of the segment 9 and 10 segmental bronchi were then completed with a tristapler.
The lateral basal segment 9 and posterior basal segment 10 of the right lower lobe were then marked by electrocautery to the visceral pleura to provide guidance for where to site the tristapler.
The resection was then completed by stapling off the marked lung segments which corresponded to segment 9 and 10 of the right lower lobe.
Initially, the stapling was towards segment 6 using a tunnelling technique.
This enabled the creation of a plane between segment 6 and segment 10.
When stapling, care must be taken to see the full length of the stapler to avoid accidental injury to important structures such as other branches of the pulmonary artery.
The branching pattern can be further delineated in this shot and nicely shows segment 6 artery passing up towards the superior segment of the right lower lobe.
Following division of the lung parenchyma here, the bronchus comes into view nicely and it can be seen that it subdivides and follows the pattern of the arteries.

Just the final parts of the stapling being shown here, demonstrating the final separation of the superior basal segment, segment 6, and the medial and anterior basal segments, segments 7 and 8, from segments 9 and 10, the lateral and the posterior basal segments.
The specimen was then removed using an endocatch bag and sent to histology for confirmation of diagnosis.

The operation was then completed by citing a single chest drain connecting to a topaz suction unit, as well as citing a paravertebral catheter and intercostal nerve blocks for the purposes of analgesia.
